data.title: freenet AG reports 1H results
data.description:
• freenet AG press release (OTCPK:FRTAF): 1H total adjusted EBITDA of EUR 257.4 million (prior year: EUR 256.1 million), with a stable adjusted EBITDA margin of 21.2% (prior year: 21.3%).
  • Revenues of EUR 1,213.1 million increased by 0.9%, postpaid service revenues by 0.6%.
  • Guidance for adjusted EBITDA (EUR 520 million to EUR 540 million) and free cash flow (EUR 300 million to EUR 320 million) for the full year confirmed.

data.title: UGI signals top-end EPS guidance of $3.15 for fiscal 2025 amid portfolio optimization and AmeriGas overhaul
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: UGI Corporation (UGI) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Robert C. Flexon highlighted that UGI delivered record year-to-date adjusted diluted earnings per share of $3.55, stating this was "up $0.33 over the prior year period." Flexon attributed the performance to contributions from all segments, particularly "strategic investments in the growth-oriented natural gas infrastructure, operating efficiencies, particularly at UGI International, the customer focus improvement... at AmeriGas and income tax credits."
  • Flexon announced that for fiscal Q3, adjusted diluted EPS was negative $0.01, compared to $0.06 in the prior year, emphasizing this reflects normal seasonal patterns.
  • UGI expects to "be at the top end of our fiscal 2025 adjusted earnings per share guidance range of $3 to $3.15," citing strong year-to-date performance and ongoing business momentum.
  • Over $600 million in year-to-date capital deployed, with "more than 80% directed to our highest risk-adjusted return businesses, the regulated Utilities and UGI energy services."
  • The Utilities segment saw "sustained customer growth of approximately 9,000 residential heating and commercial customers added this fiscal year."
  • On regulatory progress, Flexon stated, "a joint petition for approval of settlement filed on July 9... for $69.5 million in revenue increase" with new rates anticipated for fiscal Q1 2026.
  • Portfolio optimization is underway, with "definitive agreements for asset sales... expected to generate approximately $150 million in total proceeds during fiscal 2025."
  • UGI will "substantially [exit] the wholesale business" at AmeriGas, focusing on profitable segments; the wholesale business represented about 11% of LPG gallons sold in fiscal 2024 but was essentially breakeven.
  • CFO Sean P. O’Brien reported, "For the third quarter, UGI reported adjusted diluted EPS of negative $0.01 compared to positive $0.06 in the prior year period." O’Brien detailed segment results, noting declines in Utility and UGI International EBIT, and a $16 million year-over-year drop in Midstream & Marketing EBIT, offset by lower operating expenses and higher gas marketing margins.

Outlook

  • UGI expects "earnings from our underlying businesses, excluding taxes, will be largely consistent with the prior year period" for fiscal Q4.
  • Flexon reiterated, "we expect that UGI will achieve the top end of its fiscal 2025 adjusted EPS guidance range of $3 to $3.15 per share."
  • Guidance excludes "potential incremental benefits from the recently enacted One Big Beautiful Bill Act." O’Brien explained that the Act's changes to interest expense deductibility are "expected to provide additional tax expense favorability as we move forward."

Financial Results

  • Adjusted diluted EPS for Q3 was negative $0.01; year-to-date EPS reached $3.55.
  • Utilities EBIT for the quarter was $30 million, down from $39 million, with total margin up $4 million due to infrastructure investments, offset by $10 million higher operating and administrative expenses and increased depreciation.
  • Midstream & Marketing EBIT was $27 million, down $16 million, with lower margins from natural gas gathering and processing and the impact of a 2024 asset divestiture, partly offset by gas marketing.
  • UGI International reported EBIT of $43 million versus $57 million, with LPG volumes down 9% and a $19 million decline in margin, partially mitigated by lower operating expenses.
  • AmeriGas posted an operating loss of $28 million, consistent with the prior year; higher retail unit margins offset lower volumes.
  • Year-to-date, AmeriGas EBIT was up $18 million, reflecting higher margins and disciplined expense management. UGI reported free cash flow of $558 million, up 11% year-on-year, and a leverage ratio of 3.8x with $1.9 billion in liquidity at June 30, 2025.

Q&A

  • Paul Andrew Zimbardo, Jefferies: Asked about the impact of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. O’Brien responded that "the biggest impact initially will be... we have lost some of the interest deductibility, specifically at AmeriGas" and that the Act allows retroactive and ongoing removal of valuation allowances, with further benefits from bonus depreciation and R&D credits.
  • Zimbardo, Jefferies: Inquired about investment opportunities in Pennsylvania Midstream. Flexon answered, "we expect well into the double digits of NDAs with potential generators and other opportunities... So we have the right assets in the right place."
  • Zimbardo, Jefferies: Asked about multiples on divestitures. Flexon said, "we wouldn't sell any asset that would be dilutive... it's got to either be equal or better than in our own hands."
  • Gabriel Philip Moreen, Mizuho: Focused on AmeriGas metrics and LPG divestitures. Flexon highlighted safety improvements, customer service and delivery KPIs, and efficiency gains, stating, "routing and delivery... rolled out now to a handful of our locations... experiencing, call it, an 8% to 10% efficiency improvement."
  • Moreen, Mizuho: Asked about Midstream contract expiries. O’Brien responded, "No significant notable contract expiries... nothing that we anticipate where there's a significant shift."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, appreciating the detail on AmeriGas improvements and portfolio strategy, with interest in the impact of new legislation and asset optimization.
  • Management demonstrated confidence, using phrases such as "we expect," "we are focusing," and "very excited about our safety performance," while acknowledging seasonal and segment-specific headwinds. In Q&A, management offered comprehensive responses but avoided specific quantitative targets for future periods, suggesting a disciplined yet cautious optimism.
  • Compared to the prior quarter, both management and analyst sentiment remained stable, with management continuing to emphasize operational improvements and financial discipline, but with heightened focus on AmeriGas execution and regulatory/legislative developments.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance remains at $3 to $3.15 adjusted EPS, now expected at the top end versus the prior quarter's raised range.
  • Strategic updates now include the substantial exit from AmeriGas wholesale, further asset divestitures targeting $150 million in proceeds, and more specific operational KPIs, particularly for AmeriGas.
  • Analysts continued to focus on process improvements, AmeriGas turnaround, and capital allocation, with increased attention to legislative impacts.
  • Key metrics such as year-to-date EPS and capital deployment were highlighted in both quarters, but current commentary emphasized execution on divestitures and AmeriGas operational transformation.
  • Management’s tone remained confident, emphasizing results and future readiness, while analysts showed consistent interest in AmeriGas and regulatory strategy.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited warmer weather in some service territories, higher operating and administrative expenses, and the effects of structural conservation on LPG volumes as challenges.
  • UGI is addressing challenges through asset portfolio optimization, focusing on high-return businesses, and AmeriGas process improvements, including exiting the wholesale segment to improve profitability.
  • Analysts raised concerns about legislative impacts, Midstream contract expiries, and metrics for AmeriGas as it undergoes operational changes.

Final Takeaway

UGI management emphasized a record year-to-date financial performance and expects to reach the top end of its fiscal 2025 adjusted EPS guidance. Portfolio optimization, disciplined capital allocation, and operational improvements, especially at AmeriGas, remain at the forefront of the company’s strategy. Management is focused on regulatory rate case outcomes, continued asset divestitures, and preparing for the key winter season, with confidence in its ability to deliver further value as it enters fiscal 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Steris anticipates 8–9% reported revenue growth in fiscal 2026 as currency tailwinds offset tariff headwinds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: STERIS plc (STE) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Daniel A. Carestio, President and CEO, highlighted a strong start to fiscal 2026, noting "total as reported revenue grew 9%" and "constant currency organic revenue grew 8% in the quarter, driven by volume as well as 230 basis points of price." He stated, "Healthcare capital equipment revenue increased 6% for the quarter with underlying order growth of 14% and ending backlog just over $400 million." Service grew 13% and Consumables rose 5% in Healthcare. Carestio also emphasized solid performance in AST, with "constant currency organic revenue grew 10%" and Life Sciences, where "constant currency organic revenue increased 4%."
  • The CEO announced a CFO transition: "Karen and I have worked together for the past 20 years at STERIS, and we have been working behind the scenes for many years preparing her for this role."
  • Michael J. Tokich, Senior VP & CFO, stated, "Gross margin for the quarter increased 20 basis points compared with prior year to 45.3%. Positive price and productivity outpaced inflation and tariff costs. EBIT margin increased 50 basis points to 22.8% of revenue compared with the first quarter of last year due to the improvement in gross margin and operating expense leverage." He added, "Free cash flow for the first quarter of fiscal 2026 was $327 million, a very strong start to the fiscal year, driven by an increase in earnings and improvements in working capital."
  • Tokich noted, "We continued to pay down debt during the quarter, ending with $1.9 billion in total debt. Gross debt to EBITDA at quarter end was 1.2x." The company also announced its "20th consecutive year of dividend increases with a 10% increase to $0.63 per quarter."

Outlook

  • Carestio stated, "We are updating our outlook for as-reported revenue due to a significant shift in forward currency rates. We now anticipate approximately 8% to 9% revenue growth, which reflects about 200 basis points of favorable currency." He added, "Constant currency organic revenue growth is unchanged at 6% to 7%. Each segment is expected to grow constant currency organic revenue in the range of 6% to 7% for fiscal 2026."
  • The AST segment is "maintaining our outlook for the year at this time" despite a stronger-than-anticipated first quarter. "Our earnings outlook is also unchanged at $9.90 to $10.15, which now reflects $45 million in tariff costs, an increase of $15 million over last quarter."
  • The company increased its free cash flow outlook by $50 million to $820 million for fiscal 2026. "CapEx remains unchanged at $375 million."

Financial Results

  • STERIS reported "net income from continuing operations in the quarter was $231.2 million, adjusted earnings per diluted share from continuing operations was $2.34, a 15% improvement compared to the prior year."
  • Capital expenditures for the quarter totaled $94 million, and depreciation and amortization was $119 million.
  • The company continued to pay down debt, ending with $1.9 billion in total debt and a gross debt to EBITDA ratio of 1.2x.

Q&A

  • Brett Adam Fishbin, KeyBanc: Asked about the revised tariff estimate and AST guidance. Michael J. Tokich explained, "the additional tariffs that we have seen on metals... steel and aluminum went from 25% to 50%, copper went from 0 to 50%, and the EU changed from 10% to 15%." Daniel A. Carestio described the AST guidance as "general conservatism" and noted, "we feel very confident in the numbers that we're putting forward."
  • Steven McLaurin Etoch, Stephens: Asked about the bioprocessing market and Life Sciences backlog. Carestio responded, "it's been much more consistent in recent periods" and "we've seen a very good strong order intake for quite some time now."
  • Michael K. Polark, Wolfe Research: Inquired about competitor commentary and updated guidance. Carestio stated, "we feel pretty good about our position and haven't seen any slowdown."
  • Jason M. Bednar, Piper Sandler: Asked about order growth, backlog, and cash allocation. Carestio said, "orders have remained strong in both sectors," and regarding capital, "we continue to be" active in M&A but will also build cash in the short run. Tokich added on debt, "we will continue to build a cash position because we have almost no prepayable debt remaining."
  • Michael Stephen Matson, Needham & Company: Asked about Life Sciences business and free cash flow guidance. Carestio described the environment as "complicated" but expressed confidence in demand, and Tokich confirmed the cash flow guidance increase is "mainly driven by working capital...both inventory and receivables."
  • Xuesong Wang, Morgan Stanley: Asked about regulatory relief for ethylene oxide sites. Carestio explained, "we didn't apply for it because we don't feel we need it. We've been way out ahead of this going back 4 years now in terms of our facilities."
  • David Howard Windley, Jefferies: Asked about hospital outlook and FX. Matson noted, "we haven't seen any pullback, nor have we seen any pullback in current procedure volumes." Tokich stated, "we are pretty much naturally hedged."
  • Michael K. Polark, Wolfe Research: Asked about ASC buildout. Carestio responded, "whenever there's relocation of where procedures occur from a capital perspective, that's generally beneficial to us."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts consistently expressed interest in understanding tariff impacts, backlog strength, and market demand stability, pressing for clarity on guidance conservatism and macro effects, with a generally neutral-to-positive tone.
  • Management maintained a confident posture during prepared remarks and Q&A, using phrases like "we feel very confident" and "we feel pretty good about our position" but acknowledged areas of "general conservatism."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s sentiment remained stable, while analysts continued to probe for risk factors but showed less skepticism given the strong start to the year and updated guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for as-reported revenue growth increased to 8%–9% (previously 6%–7%) due to currency tailwinds, while constant currency organic revenue growth remained at 6%–7%.
  • Tariff cost estimates rose to $45 million from $30 million in the prior quarter, offset by favorable foreign currency.
  • Free cash flow outlook was raised by $50 million.
  • AST started the year stronger than expected, but management maintained a conservative full-year outlook, citing moving parts in MedTech and customer manufacturing shifts.
  • Management’s tone and confidence remained steady, with continued emphasis on backlog strength and order growth. Analysts’ primary focus continued to be on tariffs, market demand, and segment outlooks.

Risks and Concerns

  • Increased tariff exposure, specifically on metals and changes in EU tariffs, raising estimated costs for the year.
  • Ongoing monitoring of bioprocessing demand and potential shifts in customer manufacturing locations.
  • Regulatory changes around ethylene oxide were addressed, with STERIS not seeking relief due to its facilities’ compliance.
  • Analysts noted concerns about broader healthcare and Life Sciences demand, hospital reimbursement changes, and macroeconomic effects on capital deployment, with management emphasizing backlog and diversified revenue streams as mitigants.

Final Takeaway

STERIS enters fiscal 2026 with robust revenue and earnings growth, increased guidance for reported revenue, and a higher free cash flow outlook, supported by strong order intake, backlog, and stable demand across segments. The company is navigating higher tariff costs through favorable currency movements and expects steady organic growth in all segments. Leadership transition in the CFO position is planned and well-prepared, and the company remains focused on operational execution, cash generation, and strategic flexibility for M&A and shareholder returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: CommScope signals $10B CCS divestiture and projects $325M-$350M RemainCo EBITDA in 2025 as transformation accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CommScope Holding Company, Inc. (COMM) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Charles L. Treadway, President and CEO, announced that CommScope has entered into a definitive agreement to sell its CCS business to Amphenol for $10.5 billion in an all-cash transaction, with an expected close in the first half of 2026. Treadway explained, "I'm excited to announce this transformational deal that unlocks equity value, returns cash to our shareholders and strengthens the business. Our equity price was not reflective of the true value of our company. This transaction now brings improved clarity to CommScope equity value." He added that after repaying all debt and redeeming preferred equity, the company expects to have significant excess cash, which will be distributed to shareholders as a dividend within 60 to 90 days following the closing of the transaction.

  • Treadway emphasized strong performance in the ANS and RUCKUS businesses, noting, "They have been a bit slower to recover than the CCS business. However, both of these businesses have had very strong second quarters and are poised for continued strong performance and growth." He reported the second quarter 2025 LTM adjusted EBITDA for these businesses at $300 million and stated, "RemainCo is well positioned to deliver year- over-year growth in 2025 with strong cash flow generation."

  • Treadway outlined mitigation strategies for tariffs, stating, "We have developed and implemented our plan to mitigate the effect of current direct and indirect tariffs. Going forward, if tariffs remain at current levels, we feel that the net impact of tariffs on our financial results will be minimal."

  • Kyle D. Lorentzen, Executive VP & CFO, commented, "For CommScope, we reported adjusted EBITDA of $338 million for the second quarter of 2025, which increased 79% from prior year. Second quarter adjusted EBITDA results were up 41% sequentially versus the first quarter of 2025."

Outlook

  • Management raised full year CommScope adjusted EBITDA guidance to a range of $1.15 billion to $1.2 billion.

  • RemainCo, consisting of ANS and RUCKUS, is projected to deliver between $325 million to $350 million of adjusted EBITDA in 2025. Lorentzen stated, "We expect RemainCo adjusted EBITDA to be between $325 million and $350 million for 2025. Our second half RemainCo adjusted EBITDA will be down from the first half, driven by some of the onetime items we realized in the second quarter and project timing in ANS."

  • The company expects to distribute excess cash as a dividend to shareholders within 60 to 90 days post CCS transaction closing, with the exact amount and timing to be determined.

Financial Results

  • CommScope delivered net sales of $1.388 billion in the second quarter, a year-over-year increase of 32%, and adjusted EBITDA of $338 million, a year-over-year increase of 79%. Adjusted EBITDA as a percentage of revenues rose to 24.3% from the first quarter, marking the fifth consecutive sequential improvement.

  • ANS and RUCKUS segments contributed revenues of $513 million, up 58% year-over-year, and delivered $127 million of adjusted EBITDA, an increase of 326% versus the second quarter of 2024. RemainCo adjusted EBITDA as a percentage of sales was 24.7%.

  • CCS segment reported revenue of $875 million, up 20% year-over-year, and adjusted EBITDA of $211 million, up 23% year-over-year. The enterprise fiber business within CCS saw year-over-year revenue grow by 85%.

  • Cash flow from operations for the quarter was $77 million, with free cash flow of $64 million. The company ended the quarter with $571 million in global cash and total available liquidity of $991 million. Net leverage ratio ended at 6.6x.

Q&A

  • Meta A. Marshall, Morgan Stanley, asked whether ANS and RUCKUS make sense together as RemainCo and about future overhead costs. Treadway responded, "We're focused on running the businesses and closing the recently announced transaction... We've worked hard over the last 5 years to improve the positions of these businesses," while Lorentzen said, "As part of the transaction, we're going to convey or transfer a significant amount of our G&A team to Amphenol."

  • George Charles Notter, Wolfe Research, inquired about CapEx and working capital obligations for CCS and customer concentration in RemainCo. Lorentzen replied, "We're not going to provide any specifics on that... we will continue to support the business and obviously get the cash flow that comes from that business between sign and close." On customer concentration, "ANS business has higher concentration. However, the RUCKUS business... has the least amount of concentration."

  • Timothy Paul Savageaux, Northland Capital Markets, asked about the next-gen versus legacy business mix in ANS and prospects for double-digit growth. Lorentzen stated, "the majority of our revenue is coming from next-gen products in ANS, the legacy technology and business... is clearly less than 50%." On growth, "we're not here to guide to '26, but even in '25, we sort of guided toward a lower second half than first half, really driven from... project standpoint timing and some of the license revenue we get."

  • Simon Matthew Leopold, Raymond James, asked about cash flow split between RemainCo and CCS and tariff impacts on customer behavior. Lorentzen said, "that's not something that we have," and Treadway added, "our customers understood and recognize that we have a flexible global manufacturing network... there might have been some pull in maybe in the RUCKUS side because they weren't sure about what this exemption was going to do."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a positive tone, focusing on strategic fit, growth prospects, and operational details, frequently congratulating management on the transaction and results.

  • Management remained confident and optimistic during both prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing execution and strategic transformation. Treadway used confident language such as "I'm excited to announce this transformational deal" and "We are extremely pleased with the direction that ANS is headed."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed increased optimism, with fewer concerns raised about market uncertainty or operational challenges.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured the announcement of the CCS divestiture and a substantial increase in full-year adjusted EBITDA guidance.

  • Strategic focus shifted from capacity expansion and market normalization (Q1) to transformational portfolio realignment and capital returns (Q2).

  • Management confidence was elevated, supported by continued margin improvements and strong segment performance. In Q1, there was more emphasis on managing through external uncertainties, while Q2 highlighted proactive strategic moves.

  • Analysts' questions shifted from tariff impacts and customer behavior (Q1) to implications of the divestiture, RemainCo structure, and growth mix (Q2).

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the cyclical nature of the ANS business, expecting second half EBITDA to be lower due to product mix and timing.

  • Tariff risk was addressed, with management stating that mitigation plans are in place and net financial impact is expected to be minimal if tariffs remain at current levels.

  • Customer concentration in ANS was noted as higher, but management is actively managing this risk.

Final Takeaway

CommScope's Q2 2025 call was defined by the announced $10.5 billion sale of the CCS business to Amphenol, a move management described as transformational for unlocking equity value and enabling significant capital return to shareholders. ANS and RUCKUS delivered strong growth, with management projecting continued gains but cautioning on second-half cyclicality, particularly for ANS. The company raised its full-year adjusted EBITDA guidance and expects RemainCo to generate between $325 million to $350 million in 2025, positioning itself for further growth and shareholder value creation following the completion of the CCS divestiture.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: SoftBank acknowledges Stargate project requires more time
data.description:
SoftBank Group (OTCPK:SFTBY) (OTCPK:SFTBF) said it is taking longer than expected to get the Stargate project off the ground, acknowledging for the first time that the $500B AI collaboration in the U.S. with OpenAI is slowing down, 

SoftBank's CFO Yoshimitsu Goto said during an earnings briefing that all preparations are taking place at the same time. "Although it may be taking a little bit more time than we originally expected, but we [will] make sure that we will be able to make good progress on this," Goto added.

Goto added that the project progress of Stargate has been going slower than expected. "That was my feeling, my personal observation. One of the reasons is site selection. Of course, there are a lot of options, and it's been taking time to select a good site. And there are a lot of stakeholders. To build consensus, we need to have a lot of discussions and also technical issues and construction issues. There are a lot of things that we need to look at," said Goto

The company is trying to reach a consensus with partners, including Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) and Abu Dhabi’s MGX. The Japanese company expects to start “concrete” negotiations for its first Stargate datacenter site soon, according to Goto, Bloomberg News reported.

“We need to take our time to prepare a model case for Stargate,” Goto said to the analysts and reporters.

SoftBank's Founder Masayoshi Son and OpenAI Co-Founder Sam Altman introduced the Stargate project in January along with President Donald Trump, with promises to start deploying $100B “immediately” and raise that to about $500B. However, months later, it’s yet to start raising funds or building data centers.

Some of the discussions behind Stargate have slowed due to market volatility, uncertainty about U.S. trade policy and questions around the financial valuations of AI hardware, Bloomberg News reported in May. Previously, it was also reported that SoftBank had pitched the idea of taking part in a $1T AI manufacturing hub in Arizona to Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) and others.

OpenAI is backed by Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT).

Update: The story was updated with a statement from SoftBank.

data.title: NCR Voyix outlines $2.575B-$2.65B revenue outlook while advancing cloud and payments integration
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: NCR Voyix Corporation (VYX) Q2 2025

Management View

  • James G. Kelly, CEO, stated the company continued to execute on key initiatives, highlighting new customer wins in restaurant and retail, expanded adoption of the Voyix Commerce Platform (VCP), and growing interest in new cloud products and payment capabilities. He said, "We will be launching additional VCP capabilities beginning in the fourth quarter and continuing into next year. In retail, we will be launching our enterprise grocery and convenience point-of-sale, self-checkout and fuel at the NRF show in January. In restaurants, we'll be launching a new all-in-one application for labor, inventory, reporting and scheduling in the fourth quarter and our new centralized menu management solution in early 2026."
  • Kelly described the payments strategy: "We completed our pilot for Voyix Pay in the U.S. in July and are on track to complete the migration of our existing SME portfolio and sign new customers directly to the processing platform by mid-September." He also emphasized expanded acquiring capabilities in the U.K., Canada, and Latin America, enabled by Global Payments' acquisition of Worldpay.
  • On hardware, the CEO reported that "there have been no material changes to the tariff-related cost to our business, which we continue to estimate to be between $8 million and $12 million for the year."
  • Nick East, Chief Product Officer, provided product performance updates, noting "nearly 78,000 sites connected to the Voyix Commerce Platform, an increase of 16% year-over-year." He said, "In the first half of 2025, volumes were more than 50% higher than the prior year, and the VCP processed more than 500 million transaction API calls in June alone."
  • Beimnet Tadele, President of Restaurants, said, "In the second quarter, our restaurant business signed more than 200 new software and services customers... Software ARR increased 4% and total ARR increased 3% in the quarter."
  • Darren Wilson, President of Retail, reported, "Our retail business signed nearly 50 software and service customers. Our platform and payment sites increased 25% and 13%, respectively. Software ARR increased 9% and total ARR increased 6% in the quarter."
  • Brian J. Webb-Walsh, CFO, stated, "For the quarter, we delivered revenue and adjusted EBITDA in line with expectations. Total revenue of $666 million declined 8% due to continued softness in hardware sales. Recurring revenue increased 4% to $422 million and as a percent of total revenue improved over 700 basis points to 63%."

Outlook

  • Webb-Walsh reiterated, "We continue to expect revenue to range from $2.575 billion to $2.65 billion and adjusted EBITDA to range from $420 million to $445 million. Non-GAAP diluted EPS is expected to be between $0.75 and $0.80, and adjusted free cash flow is expected to be between $170 million and $190 million."
  • Kelly confirmed, "Since our call in May, there have been no material changes to the tariff-related cost to our business, which we continue to estimate to be between $8 million and $12 million for the year."

Financial Results

  • Total revenue for the quarter was $666 million, with recurring revenue at $422 million, representing 63% of total revenue. Adjusted EBITDA reached $95 million, and margin expanded to 14.3%.
  • Platform sites increased 16% to 78,000, while payment sites rose 3% to 8,400.
  • Restaurant segment recurring revenue was $143 million, and total segment revenue was $205 million. Segment adjusted EBITDA was $68 million with a margin of 33.2%.
  • Retail segment recurring revenue was $277 million. Total segment revenue was $454 million. Segment adjusted EBITDA was $81 million, and margin was 17.8%.
  • Adjusted free cash flow was $37 million for the quarter before considering restructuring, tax, and product investments. CapEx was $42 million for the quarter.

Q&A

  • Daniel Rock Perlin, RBC Capital Markets: Asked about demand environment and visibility into free cash flow ramp in the second half. Kelly responded, "I would say we have not seen any pullback from customers... So I don't see necessarily a slowdown in terms of what they're looking to do to modernize their infrastructure." Webb-Walsh added, "Q2 was in line with our expectations on free cash flow. And to your point, we tend to generate our free cash flow in the second half."
  • Isaac Arthur Sellhausen, Oppenheimer: Inquired about restaurant EBITDA margin strength and tariff exposure. Webb-Walsh replied, "Good software and services growth and recurring revenue growth in restaurants is helping margin... We see restaurants finishing the year about 32%." On tariffs, Kelly stated, "We feel comfortable in the range that we just outlined... this is not going to end sometime soon."
  • William Alfred Nance, Goldman Sachs: Asked about cost initiatives and a contract renewal. Webb-Walsh shared, "Our cost program for this year, we sized it at $100 million, 2/3 is around vendor spend, 1/3 around our own labor. That program is largely executed or being executed. About 40% of the savings hit in the first half and about 60% will hit in the second half."
  • Kartik Mehta, Northcoast Research: Asked about payments and competition in restaurants. Kelly explained, "We're not on that system until, I guess, the fourth quarter... I would see it ramping into next year."
  • John Patrick McShane, Stifel: Asked about ARR trends and payment growth. Kelly noted, "ARR -- software ARR was something around $700 million. It's approaching $800 million today."
  • Alexander Blake Neumann, Stephens: Sought color on ARR backlog and forward software growth. Kelly and Wilson both indicated positive momentum and a shift towards recurring revenue.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a generally neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on execution, demand environment, cost control, and future visibility. Their primary concerns centered on the ramp in free cash flow, margin performance, and competitive positioning in payments.
  • Management's tone was confident, emphasizing progress on platform adoption, payments integration, and cost actions. Kelly used phrases like "I remain encouraged by the progress" and "I'm increasingly convinced that NCR Voyix can advance the foundational strengths."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management sustained a similar level of engagement and cautious optimism, with a continued focus on cost controls, margin improvement, and product innovation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for revenue, adjusted EBITDA, non-GAAP diluted EPS, and adjusted free cash flow remains unchanged from the previous quarter.
  • Recurring revenue and ARR growth rates improved, with platform and payment site gains accelerating in both restaurant and retail segments.
  • Management's confidence in product innovation, payments integration, and global service expansion is more pronounced, with increased detail on forthcoming launches and customer wins.
  • Analyst focus remained on execution, margin delivery, cash flow, and payment strategy, consistent with the prior quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariff exposure remains a key challenge, with management maintaining the estimated cost range of $8 million to $12 million and monitoring the situation closely.
  • Hardware sales softness continues to impact total revenue, though recurring revenue and platform growth offset some effects.
  • Analyst questions highlighted the need for sustained cost controls, execution on payment integration, and visible progress on software ARR and platform migration.

Final Takeaway

NCR Voyix’s management emphasized ongoing momentum in platform adoption, payments integration, and software-driven recurring revenue, while reiterating unchanged full-year guidance. Expansion of cloud-native applications, enhanced payment capabilities, and a disciplined approach to cost control are positioned as key drivers for future growth, with management expressing confidence in improved performance and customer engagement across core business segments.
